title: Directional Isoperimetric Inequalities and Rational Homotopy Invariants
overall_summary: The paper proves sharp, anisotropic estimates for a second-order linking invariant of maps from an n-dimensional ellipsoid to a wedge of three spheres, using direction-dependent isoperimetric inequalities and a direction-dependent coarea inequality. Its central technical contribution is a filling theorem that controls each coordinate-projection volume of a filling chain separately, first for absolute and relative cycles in rectangles and then for cycles in ellipsoids via a double-of-a-rectangle model. These estimates yield Theorem 1, an upper bound for the second-order linking invariant under a k1-dilation hypothesis, and Theorem 3, a lower bound for the k-dilation of degree-D maps between ellipsoids in terms of axis ratios. For the original rectangle problem, the most useful ingredients are the directional filling inequalities in rectangles, the directional coarea inequality, and the strategy of composing a degree-one map with a carefully constructed test map to a wedge of spheres and comparing a homotopy-invariant lower bound with a dilation-dependent upper bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Directional volume definition and comparison estimate
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C be an integral Lipschitz m-chain in R^n. For an m-tuple J of distinct elements of {1,...,n}, let P(J) be the corresponding coordinate m-plane. The J-volume Vol_J(C) is the m-dimensional volume of the projection of C to P(J), counted with geometric multiplicity; equivalently Vol_J(C) = РѕФ_C |T C_x ┬и P(J)| dvol(x), where T C_x is the oriented unit simple m-vector tangent to C at almost every point and P(J) is the oriented unit coordinate m-vector. Then Vol(C) РЅц ╬Б_J Vol_J(C) РЅц binom(n,m) Vol(C), where the sum ranges over all coordinate m-tuples J.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: At almost every point of C, expand the unit simple m-vector T C_x in the orthonormal basis of coordinate m-vectors. The squared coefficients sum to 1. Therefore the sum of the absolute values of the coefficients is at least 1 and at most the number binom(n,m) of coefficients. Integrating this pointwise inequality over C gives the two-sided comparison between total m-volume and the sum of directional m-volumes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1 (coarea inequality, as stated in the introduction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F : (M^n,g) Рєњ (N^q,h) be a CРѕъ map between Riemannian manifolds with Lipschitz constant L. Then F has a regular fiber whose (nРѕњq)-dimensional volume is at most L^q Vol(M,g) / Vol(N,h).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the coarea formula: the integral over N of the volumes of the fibers F^{-1}(y) equals the integral over M of the q-dimensional Jacobian of dF. Since F is L-Lipschitz, the q-Jacobian is bounded by L^q at every point. Hence the average fiber volume over N is at most L^q Vol(M)/Vol(N), so some regular fiber satisfies the same bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Loomis-Whitney directional estimate (quoted background)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If U is an open subset of R^n and, for each coordinate (nРѕњ1)-tuple J, A_J denotes the (nРѕњ1)-volume of the projection of U onto the coordinate hyperplane P(J), then Vol(U) РЅц (╬а_J A_J)^{1/(nРѕњ1)}, where the product is over the n coordinate hyperplanes. As an immediate corollary, if H is a closed embedded hypersurface in R^n enclosing a region of volume V, then V РЅц (╬а_J Vol_J(H))^{1/(nРѕњ1)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes the Loomis-Whitney theorem rather than proving it. The hypersurface corollary follows by observing that the projection volume of the enclosed region onto a coordinate hyperplane is bounded by the corresponding directional volume of its boundary, up to the convention used in the paper for geometric multiplicity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Federer-Fleming isoperimetric inequality (quoted background)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If z is a closed k-dimensional integral cycle in R^n, then there is a (k+1)-dimensional integral chain y in R^n with Рѕѓy = z and |y| РЅц C(n) |z|^{(k+1)/k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this classical theorem as the non-directional model for filling cycles of arbitrary codimension. No proof is included in the paper; it is used as background motivation for the directional filling estimates developed later.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2 / Isoperimetric inequality in an ellipse (quoted from Guth [2])
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 РЅц E_1 РЅц ... РЅц E_n. If z is an integral m-cycle in E, then z bounds an integral (m+1)-chain y in E satisfying Vol(y) РЅц C(n) [E_{m+1} + E_{nРѕњm}] Vol(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from Guth [2] and does not reprove it at this point. Later, after proving the directional isoperimetric inequality in an ellipse, the paper notes that summing the directional estimates recovers this ordinary volume bound because for an (m+1)-tuple I, its smallest element is at most nРѕњm and the smallest missing element e satisfies eРѕњ1 РЅц m+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1 (definition, well-definedness, and homotopy invariance of L2)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 2 РЅц k_1 РЅц k_2 РЅц k_3 and n+2 = k_1+k_2+k_3. For a smooth map F : S^n Рєњ S^{k_1} Рѕе S^{k_2} Рѕе S^{k_3} and regular non-basepoint values q_i Рѕѕ S^{k_i}, define z_1 = F^{-1}(q_1), choose an integral chain y_1 with Рѕѓy_1 = z_1, define z_2 = y_1 РѕЕ F^{-1}(q_2) after putting y_1 in general position, choose y_2 with Рѕѓy_2 = z_2, and let ¤ђ be the retraction of the wedge to S^{k_3}. Since ¤ђРѕўF maps Рѕѓy_2 to the basepoint, it has a well-defined degree on y_2; this integer is L2(F). For arbitrary continuous F, define L2(F) by first homotoping F to a smooth map for which the chosen q_i are regular. Proposition 1.1 states that L2(F) is independent of y_1, y_2, the smoothing/homotopy used to define it, and the choices of q_i; moreover L2 is a homotopy invariant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Independence of y_2: if y_2 and y_2' both fill z_2, their difference is a k_3-cycle in S^n; it is exact, so the degree of ¤ђРѕўF on that difference is zero. Independence of y_1: if y_1Рѕњy_1' bounds A, then B = A РѕЕ F^{-1}(q_2) has boundary z_2Рѕњz_2'. Choosing y_2' = y_2РѕњB gives fillings for the two choices, and the degree difference is the degree of ¤ђРѕўF on B; but B lies in F^{-1}(q_2), and q_2 lies in the S^{k_2} summand, so ¤ђРѕўF(B) is the basepoint and contributes zero. Homotopy invariance: for a homotopy F : S^n├Ќ[0,1] Рєњ wedge, use the fiber F^{-1}(q_1) as a homology between the endpoint fibers, fill the resulting cycle by a chain y_1 in S^n├Ќ[0,1], intersect y_1 with F^{-1}(q_2), fill again by y_2, and observe that ¤ђРѕўF(y_2) gives a homology between the endpoint k_3-cycles in S^{k_3}; hence their degrees agree. Independence of q_i follows by using a diffeomorphism of the wedge, homotopic to the identity, carrying one set of non-basepoint choices to another.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Unlabelled product calculation for the second-order linking invariant
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f : S^n Рєњ S^{k_1} Рѕе S^{nРѕњk_1+1} and g : S^{nРѕњk_1+1} Рєњ S^{k_2} Рѕе S^{k_3} be continuous maps, with n+2 = k_1+k_2+k_3 and 2 РЅц k_1 РЅц k_2 РЅц k_3. Let g_+ : S^{k_1} Рѕе S^{nРѕњk_1+1} Рєњ S^{k_1} Рѕе S^{k_2} Рѕе S^{k_3} be the map equal to the identity on S^{k_1} and equal to g on S^{nРѕњk_1+1}. Then L2(g_+Рѕўf) = L(f) L(g), where L denotes the ordinary first-order linking invariant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let F = g_+Рѕўf. The first fiber z_1 = F^{-1}(q_1) is f^{-1}(q_1), and choose y_1 with Рѕѓy_1 = z_1. Let w = g^{-1}(q_2) in S^{nРѕњk_1+1} and choose v with Рѕѓv = w. Then z_2 = y_1 РѕЕ f^{-1}(w), and a convenient filling is y_2 = y_1 РѕЕ f^{-1}(v). The final zero-cycle is z_3 = y_2 РѕЕ F^{-1}(q_3) = y_1 РѕЕ f^{-1}(v РѕЕ g^{-1}(q_3)). The intersection v РѕЕ g^{-1}(q_3) is a signed set of L(g) points, and for each such point p, the signed count y_1 РѕЕ f^{-1}(p) is L(f). Thus the total signed count is L(f)L(g).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2 (bounding L2 by isoperimetric constants and k1-dilation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 2 РЅц k_1 РЅц k_2 РЅц k_3 and n = k_1+k_2+k_3Рѕњ2. Let F : (S^n,g) Рєњ (S^{k_1} Рѕе S^{k_2} Рѕе S^{k_3}, h_1Рѕеh_2Рѕеh_3) be a map whose k_1-dilation is at most L^{k_1}. Let Iso_j(g) denote the least constant I such that every integral j-cycle in (S^n,g) bounds an integral (j+1)-chain of volume at most I times the volume of the cycle. Then |L2(F)| РЅц Iso_{nРѕњk_1}(g) Iso_{k_3Рѕњ1}(g) Vol(g) L^{n+2} Vol(h_1)^{-1} Vol(h_2)^{-1} Vol(h_3)^{-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the coarea inequality to choose q_1 Рѕѕ S^{k_1} so that z_1 = F^{-1}(q_1) has volume at most L^{k_1} Vol(g)/Vol(h_1). Fill z_1 by y_1 with volume at most Iso_{nРѕњk_1}(g) times that. Apply coarea to F restricted to y_1 and the S^{k_2} summand to choose q_2 so that z_2 = y_1 РѕЕ F^{-1}(q_2) has volume at most Iso_{nРѕњk_1}(g) Vol(g) L^{k_1+k_2} Vol(h_1)^{-1}Vol(h_2)^{-1}. Fill z_2 by y_2 using Iso_{k_3Рѕњ1}(g). Finally, the degree of ¤ђРѕўF on y_2 is bounded in absolute value by L^{k_3} Vol(y_2)/Vol(h_3). Multiplying these estimates gives the stated bound. The use of L^{k_i} for i РЅЦ 1 is justified by the hypothesis on k_1-dilation together with k_i РЅЦ k_1, which controls higher-dimensional Jacobians in the required directions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition on isoperimetric constants of ellipses (quoted from Guth [2])
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 РЅц ... РЅц E_n. For 0 РЅц k РЅц nРѕњ1, the k-dimensional isoperimetric constant Iso_k(E) is comparable, up to multiplicative constants depending only on n, to E_{k+1}+E_{nРѕњk}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this proposition from Guth [2]. It is used by inserting the estimate into Proposition 1.2. The two terms correspond to the two different types of hard-to-fill cycles in an ellipse: for k large, small equators are hardest; for k small, large equators are hardest.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3 (basic L2 bound on an ellipse from ordinary isoperimetry)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 2 РЅц k_1 РЅц k_2 РЅц k_3 and n+2 = k_1+k_2+k_3. Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 РЅц ... РЅц E_n. Let F : E Рєњ S^{k_1} Рѕе S^{k_2} Рѕе S^{k_3} be a map to a wedge of unit spheres with k_1-dilation at most L^{k_1}. If k_3 < (n+1)/2, then |L2(F)| РЅц C(n) E_{nРѕњk_1+1} E_{nРѕњk_3+1} Vol(E) L^{n+2}. If k_3 РЅЦ (n+1)/2, then |L2(F)| РЅц C(n) E_{nРѕњk_1+1} E_{k_3} Vol(E) L^{n+2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Proposition 1.2 to the ellipsoidal metric and use Iso_j(E) РЅЃ E_{j+1}+E_{nРѕњj}. For the first filling, j = nРѕњk_1, so Iso_{nРѕњk_1}(E) РЅЃ E_{nРѕњk_1+1}+E_{k_1}; since nРѕњk_1+1 = k_2+k_3Рѕњ1 is at least k_1, this is bounded by a dimensional constant times E_{nРѕњk_1+1}. For the second filling, j = k_3Рѕњ1, so Iso_{k_3Рѕњ1}(E) РЅЃ E_{k_3}+E_{nРѕњk_3+1}; this is controlled by E_{nРѕњk_3+1} when k_3 < (n+1)/2 and by E_{k_3} when k_3 РЅЦ (n+1)/2. The target spheres have unit volume, so their volumes are absorbed into C(n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1 (directional filling of absolute cycles in a rectangle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R_1]├Ќ...├Ќ[0,R_n] be a rectangle with R_1 РЅц ... РЅц R_n. If z is an m-dimensional integral cycle in R, then there is an integral (m+1)-chain y in R with Рѕѓy = z such that for every coordinate (m+1)-tuple I, if i is the smallest element of I and IРѕњi denotes the m-tuple obtained by removing i, then Vol_I(y) РЅц R_i Vol_{IРѕњi}(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proceed by induction on n. Let ¤ђ be projection to the face {x_1=0}. There is a straight-line homology from z to ¤ђ(z) obtained by moving each point parallel to the x_1-axis; this homology has (1РѕфJ)-volume at most R_1 Vol_J(z) for each J not containing 1 and has I-volume zero for I not containing 1. The projected cycle ¤ђ(z) lies in the (nРѕњ1)-dimensional rectangle {0}├Ќ[0,R_2]├Ќ...├Ќ[0,R_n]. By the induction hypothesis, ¤ђ(z) bounds y' there with Vol_I(y') РЅц R_i Vol_{IРѕњi}(¤ђ(z)) РЅц R_i Vol_{IРѕњi}(z) for coordinate tuples I not containing 1. Combining the straight homology with y' gives a filling y of z satisfying all the required directional estimates. The paper emphasizes the useful consequence that some directional volumes of z, namely those whose tuple contains 1, are not needed to bound the total volume of the constructed filling.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1 (pushing a relative cycle that avoids the top face)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R_1]├ЌR' with R' = [0,R_2]├Ќ...├Ќ[0,R_n]. Suppose z is an m-chain in R with Рѕѓz contained in РѕѓR, and suppose Рѕѓz does not intersect the top face {R_1}├ЌR'. Then there is an (m+1)-chain y in R and an m-chain B in РѕѓR such that Рѕѓy = z+B and: (1) if I is an (m+1)-tuple containing 1, then Vol_I(y) РЅц R_1 Vol_{IРѕњ1}(z); (2) if I is an (m+1)-tuple not containing 1, then Vol_I(y)=0; (3) if J is an m-tuple not containing 1, then Vol_J(B) РЅц Vol_J(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let I : z Рєњ R be the identity map with coordinate functions I_1,...,I_n. Define f : z├Ќ[0,1] Рєњ R by f_1(x,t)=t I_1(x) and f_i(x,t)=I_i(x) for iРЅа1, and set y=f(z├Ќ[0,1]). The boundary of y consists of z, the projection f(z├Ќ{0}) into the bottom face, and the swept image f(Рѕѓz├Ќ[0,1]). Because Рѕѓz lies in РѕѓR and avoids the top face, this swept boundary lies in the sides and bottom of R; hence Рѕѓy=z+B with BРіѓРѕѓR. The chain y lies in [0,R_1]├Ќ¤ђ(z), where ¤ђ is projection onto R', giving the stated estimates for Vol_I(y). The boundary chain B has one piece coming from f(Рѕѓz├Ќ[0,1]), whose J-volume is zero when J does not contain 1, and one piece equal to Рѕњ¤ђ(z), whose J-volume is at most Vol_J(z).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2 (directional filling/pushing of relative cycles in a rectangle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R_1]├Ќ...├Ќ[0,R_n] be a rectangle with R_1 РЅц ... РЅц R_n. Suppose z is an m-dimensional relative integral cycle in R, meaning РѕѓzРіѓРѕѓR. Then there is an (m+1)-chain y in R and an m-chain B contained in РѕѓR such that Рѕѓy = z+B and the following estimates hold. For every coordinate (m+1)-tuple I, let e be the smallest number in {1,...,n} not contained in I; then Vol_I(y) РЅц C(n) ╬Б_{d=1}^{eРѕњ1} R_d Vol_{IРѕњd}(z), where d<e implies dРѕѕI. In addition, if J is an m-tuple not containing 1 and e is the smallest number in {2,...,n} not contained in J, then Vol_J(B) РЅц C(n) [Vol_J(z) + ╬Б_{d=1}^{eРѕњ1} R_1^{-1} R_d Vol_{(1РѕфJ)Рѕњd}(z)], where (1РѕфJ)Рѕњd means remove d from the tuple {1}РѕфJ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n and uses Lemma 2.1 plus slicing. For hРѕѕ[0,R_1], let z_h = zРѕЕ{x_1=h}. By the coarea inequality, choose h such that for every (mРѕњ1)-tuple K in {2,...,n}, Vol_K(z_h) РЅц C(n) R_1^{-1} Vol_{1РѕфK}(z). Decompose z as z = z_1+z_2, where z_2=[0,R_1]├Ќz_h and z_1=zРѕњz_2; then split z_1 into an upper part z_+ and lower part z_Рѕњ relative to the slice. Each of z_+ and z_Рѕњ is a relative cycle avoiding one of the top/bottom faces, so Lemma 2.1 fills or pushes it to the boundary. The directional volumes of the artificial slice-cylinder [0,R_1]├Ќz_h are controlled by the choice of h, so the resulting y_1 and B_1 satisfy the desired estimates. For z_2, apply the induction hypothesis in the (nРѕњ1)-rectangle [0,R_2]├Ќ...├Ќ[0,R_n] to fill z_h by y_h with boundary z_h+B_h and directional bounds. Set y_2=[0,R_1]├Ќy_h. If an (m+1)-tuple I contains 1, Vol_I(y_2)=R_1 Vol_{IРѕњ1}(y_h), which is controlled by the inductive estimate and the chosen slice bounds; if I omits 1, Vol_I(y_2)=0. The boundary contribution B_2=[0,R_1]├ЌB_hРѕњ{0}├Ќy_h+{R_1}├Ќy_h lies in РѕѓR, and its J-volumes for J not containing 1 are controlled by Vol_J(y_h). Adding y_1+y_2 and B_1+B_2 completes the induction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3 (directional isoperimetric inequality in an ellipse)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 РЅц E_1 РЅц ... РЅц E_n. Directional volumes in E are defined by fixing a C(n)-bilipschitz identification of E with the double of the rectangle [0,E_1]├Ќ...├Ќ[0,E_n], splitting a chain into its northern and southern pieces, and adding the corresponding directional volumes in the two copies of the rectangle. If z is an m-dimensional integral cycle in E, then z bounds an integral (m+1)-chain y in E with the following estimates: for every coordinate (m+1)-tuple I, let i be the smallest element of I and let e be the smallest element of {1,...,n} not in I; then Vol_I(y) РЅц C(n) [E_i Vol_{IРѕњi}(z) + ╬Б_{d=1}^{eРѕњ1} E_d Vol_{IРѕњd}(z)].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write z=z_N+z_S according to the northern and southern halves of the double rectangle. Treat z_S as a relative cycle in the southern rectangle and apply Proposition 2.2 to get y_S with Рѕѓy_S=z_S+B, where B lies on the equatorial boundary, and with directional estimates for y_S and for those directional volumes of B whose tuples omit 1. Then z_NРѕњB is an absolute cycle in the northern rectangle. Apply Proposition 2.1 to fill it by y_N, giving Vol_I(y_N) РЅц E_i [Vol_{IРѕњi}(z_N)+Vol_{IРѕњi}(B)], where i is the smallest element of I. Since IРѕњi never contains 1, the boundary estimates for B from Proposition 2.2 apply. If i>1, then IРѕњi also misses 2, so the relevant estimate gives Vol_{IРѕњi}(B) controlled by Vol_{IРѕњi}(z_S), leading to Vol_I(y_N) РЅ▓ E_i Vol_{IРѕњi}(z). If i=1, the estimate for B gives Vol_{IРѕњ1}(B) РЅ▓ E_1^{-1} ╬Б_{d=1}^{eРѕњ1} E_d Vol_{IРѕњd}(z), and multiplication by E_1 gives the second term in the claimed bound. Finally set y=y_N+y_S; then Рѕѓy=z and combining the bounds for y_N and y_S yields the proposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 (exterior algebra estimate used in directional coarea)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be an m-dimensional plane in R^n, and let J be a coordinate k-tuple, also viewed as the corresponding unit coordinate k-vector. Then sup_{QРіѓP, dim Q=k} |J┬иQ| = (╬Б_{JРіѓI} |I┬иP|^2)^{1/2}, where Q ranges over oriented k-planes contained in P, I ranges over coordinate m-tuples containing J, and P and I are also viewed as unit simple m-vectors.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Both sides are invariant under rotations that fix the coordinate k-plane J. Use such a rotation to put PРѕЕJ^РіЦ in coordinate position, say equal to the coordinate (mРѕњk)-plane K disjoint from J. Let Q_0=PРѕЕK^РіЦ, so P=Q_0РіЋK. For any k-plane QРіѓP, write a unit k-vector for Q as a wedge of vectors v_i=u_i+w_i with u_iРѕѕQ_0 and w_iРѕѕK. When taking the inner product with J, all terms involving any w_i vanish, so |J┬иQ|РЅц|J┬иQ_0|, and equality is attained at Q_0. On the right-hand side, the only coordinate m-tuple I containing J with nonzero contribution is I=JРѕфK; hence the right-hand side equals |JРѕДK ┬и Q_0РѕДK|=|J┬иQ_0|.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1 (directional coarea inequality)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let y be a CРѕъ m-chain in R^n, and let F : y Рєњ (N^q,h) be a CРѕъ map to a q-dimensional Riemannian manifold whose q-dilation is at most ╬Џ. Let k=mРѕњq. Then there is a fiber z=F^{-1}(p), pРѕѕN, such that for every coordinate k-tuple J, Vol_J(z) РЅц C(n) ╬Џ Vol(N,h)^{-1} ╬Б_{JРіѓI} Vol_I(y), where the sum is over all coordinate m-tuples I containing J.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the general coarea formula with a weight G. At points where Jac dFРЅа0, the kernel of dF is a k-plane; let V(F) be its unit k-vector. Choose G=|J┬иV(F)|. Then the left-hand side of coarea, integrated over N, is РѕФ_N Vol_J(F^{-1}(p)) dp. The right-hand side is РѕФ_y Jac(dF)|J┬иV(F)| dvol РЅц ╬Џ РѕФ_y |J┬иV(F)| dvol. The k-plane V(F) lies inside the tangent m-plane Ty, so by Lemma 3.1, |J┬иV(F)|РЅц(╬Б_{JРіѓI}|I┬иTy|^2)^{1/2}РЅц╬Б_{JРіѓI}|I┬иTy|. Integrating gives РѕФ_N Vol_J(F^{-1}(p)) dp РЅц ╬Џ ╬Б_{JРіѓI} Vol_I(y). Since there are only finitely many J, choose one p for which all these inequalities hold simultaneously, losing only a dimensional constant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (refined upper bound for L2 under k1-dilation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 2 РЅц k_1 РЅц k_2 РЅц k_3 and n+2 = k_1+k_2+k_3. Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 РЅц ... РЅц E_n. Let F : E Рєњ S^{k_1} Рѕе S^{k_2} Рѕе S^{k_3} be a map to a wedge of unit spheres with k_1-dilation at most L^{k_1}. Then |L2(F)| РЅц C(n) E_{nРѕњk_1+1} E_{k_3} Vol(E) L^{n+2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First choose q_1РѕѕS^{k_1} by coarea so that z_1=F^{-1}(q_1) has volume at most C(n)Vol(E)L^{k_1}; consequently every directional volume of z_1 is bounded by the same quantity. Fill z_1 by y_1 using Proposition 2.3. For an (nРѕњk_1+1)-tuple I, with i the smallest element of I and e the smallest missing element, this gives Vol_I(y_1) РЅц C(n)Vol(E)L^{k_1}(E_i+E_{eРѕњ1}). Next choose q_2РѕѕS^{k_2} using the directional coarea inequality applied to F restricted to y_1. If J is a coordinate tuple of dimension nРѕњk_1+1Рѕњk_2, define f to be the (k_2+1)-st smallest element not in J. Since each relevant I is obtained from J by adding k_2 elements, the smallest missing element e of I is at most f, and since |I|=nРѕњk_1+1 the smallest element i of I is at most k_1. Thus z_2=y_1РѕЕF^{-1}(q_2) satisfies Vol_J(z_2) РЅц C(n)Vol(E)L^{k_1+k_2}(E_{k_1}+E_{fРѕњ1}). Fill z_2 by y_2 using Proposition 2.3. For a k_3-tuple K, let k be its smallest element and g its smallest missing element. Substitution of the z_2 estimate yields terms of the four forms E_k E_{k_1}, E_k E_{f(KРѕњk)Рѕњ1}, E_c E_{k_1}, and E_c E_{f(KРѕњc)Рѕњ1} for c<g. The paper checks each using only the ordering of the axes and the relations among k_i: kРЅцnРѕњk_3+1 for the first term; for the second, fРЅцnРѕњk_1+2 and either kРЅцk_3 or else kРЅЦk_3+1РЅЦk_2+1 implies f=k_2+1; for the third, gРЅцk_3+1 so cРЅцk_3; for the fourth, KРѕњc has k_3Рѕњ1 elements so fРЅцk_2+k_3=nРѕњk_1+2 and again cРЅцk_3. In all cases the product is bounded by E_{nРѕњk_1+1}E_{k_3}. Therefore Vol(y_2) РЅц C(n)Vol(E)L^{k_1+k_2}E_{nРѕњk_1+1}E_{k_3}. Finally choose q_3РѕѕS^{k_3} by coarea; the resulting zero-dimensional cycle z_3=y_2РѕЕF^{-1}(q_3) has at most C(n)Vol(E)L^{n+2}E_{nРѕњk_1+1}E_{k_3} points counted with multiplicity, and its signed count is L2(F).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 (Lipschitz map from an ellipse to a glued two-sphere wedge with linking invariant 1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with axes E_0РЅц...РЅцE_n. Let d,eРЅЦ2 satisfy n+1=d+e. Construct a space X by taking S^d and S^e and identifying two antipodal points p,q on S^d with a basepoint * on S^e. Put on S^d the ellipsoidal metric E[d] with axes E_0РЅц...РЅцE_d, with p and q the two tips in the E_d-direction. Put on S^e the metric obtained by one-point compactifying the rectangle with side lengths E_{nРѕњe+1},...,E_n, with the compactification point as basepoint. Then there is a map F:EРєњX with linking invariant 1 and Lipschitz constant at most C(n), and F sends the two tips of E to the basepoint of X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Identify E up to C(n)-bilipschitz equivalence with the double of the rectangle [0,E_1]├Ќ...├Ќ[0,E_n]. Define an open set U shaped like S^{nРѕњe}├ЌB^e as the double of ╬а_{i=1}^{nРѕњe}[0,E_i]├Ќ╬а_{i=nРѕњe+1}^{n}[(1/3)E_i,(2/3)E_i]. On U, project to the last e coordinates and map the resulting e-rectangle by a degree-one Lipschitz map to the compactified S^e, sending the boundary to the basepoint. Define V using the annular region in the last e coordinates between a larger rectangle ╬а[(1/10)E_i,(9/10)E_i] and the inner rectangle used for U; topologically VРЅЃS^{eРѕњ1}├ЌS^{dРѕњ1}├Ќ[0,1], and up to C(n)-bilipschitz equivalence it is Core├ЌE[dРѕњ1]├Ќ[0,E_d]. On V, project to E[dРѕњ1]├Ќ[0,E_d] and map degree one and C(n)-Lipschitz to E[d]=S^d, taking the two boundary components to the two tips p,q, which become the basepoint in X. Map the complement of UРѕфV to the basepoint. A generic point in S^e has preimage a core S^{nРѕњe} in U, and a generic point in S^d has preimage a core S^{eРѕњ1} in V; these core spheres link once in E, so the linking invariant is 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2 (existence of maps with large second-order linking invariant)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 2 РЅц k_1 РЅц k_2 РЅц k_3 and n+2 = k_1+k_2+k_3. Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 РЅц ... РЅц E_n. If L > C(n) E_1^{-1}, then there is a map ╬д : E Рєњ S^{k_1} Рѕе S^{k_2} Рѕе S^{k_3} to a wedge of unit spheres with Lipschitz constant L, up to changing the dimensional constants, and L2(╬д) РЅЦ c(n) E_{nРѕњk_1+1} E_{k_3} Vol(E) L^{n+2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Lemma 5.1 first with e=k_1, so the target X is obtained by gluing a sphere with ellipsoidal metric E[nРѕњk_1+1] to an S^{k_1} carrying the compactified rectangle metric with sides E_{nРѕњk_1+1},...,E_n. Apply Lemma 5.1 a second time to the ellipsoid E[nРѕњk_1+1] with e=k_2; the resulting space X' glues an S^{k_3} with metric E[k_3] to an S^{k_2} carrying the compactified rectangle metric with sides E_{nРѕњk_1Рѕњk_2+2},...,E_{nРѕњk_1+1}, i.e. E_{k_3},...,E_{nРѕњk_1+1}. Because the second map sends tips to the basepoint, it extends over X by acting as the identity on the S^{k_1} summand. The composition ╬д_1:EРєњY=X'РѕеS^{k_1} has L2(╬д_1)=1 by the product calculation for L2. Now construct a map ╬▒:YРєњS^{k_1}РѕеS^{k_2}РѕеS^{k_3} to unit spheres, with Lipschitz constant L, that on the S^{k_i} summand has degree D_i. The paper asserts such ╬▒ can be chosen with D_1 РЅЦ c(n) E_{nРѕњk_1+1}...E_n L^{k_1}, D_2 РЅЦ c(n) E_{k_3}...E_{nРѕњk_1+1} L^{k_2}, and D_3 РЅЦ c(n) E_1...E_{k_3} L^{k_3}. Then ╬д=╬▒Рѕў╬д_1 has Lipschitz constant at most C(n)L and L2(╬д)=D_1D_2D_3. The product of the three displayed degree lower bounds is c(n) E_{k_3}E_{nРѕњk_1+1}(E_1...E_n)L^{n+2}, and E_1...E_n is comparable to Vol(E) up to C(n), giving the theorem after adjusting constants.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 (k-dilation lower bound for degree-D maps between ellipses)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E and E' be n-dimensional ellipses with principal axes E_0РЅц...РЅцE_n and E'_0РЅц...РЅцE'_n, respectively. Set Q_i=E'_i/E_i. Suppose ╬д:EРєњE' has degree D. If 2РЅцk_1РЅцk_2РЅцk_3, n+2=k_1+k_2+k_3, and kРЅцk_1, then Dil_k(╬д) > c(n) [|D| Q_{nРѕњk_1+1} Q_{k_3} Q_1...Q_n]^{k/(n+2)}. In the special case n=4 and k=k_1=k_2=k_3=2, this gives Dil_2(╬д) РЅ│ |D|^{1/3} Q_1^{1/3} Q_2^{2/3} Q_3^{2/3} Q_4^{1/3}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Theorem 2 on the target ellipse E' to choose, for large L, a map F:E'РєњS^{k_1}РѕеS^{k_2}РѕеS^{k_3} with Lipschitz constant L and L2(F) РЅЦ c(n) E'_{nРѕњk_1+1} E'_{k_3} E'_1...E'_n L^{n+2}. Since ╬д has degree D, the composition FРѕў╬д has |L2(FРѕў╬д)| at least |D| times this lower bound. On the other hand, if ╬┤=Dil_k(╬д), then because kРЅцk_1, the k_1-dilation of FРѕў╬д is at most (╬┤^{1/k}L)^{k_1}; this is the singular-value monotonicity that converts a k-dilation bound into a higher-dimensional dilation bound. Apply Theorem 1 to FРѕў╬д as a map from E, with effective parameter ╬┤^{1/k}L, to get |L2(FРѕў╬д)| РЅц C(n) E_{nРѕњk_1+1} E_{k_3} E_1...E_n L^{n+2} ╬┤^{(n+2)/k}. Comparing lower and upper bounds, canceling L^{n+2}, and rewriting the ratio of primed to unprimed axes as Q_i gives the stated inequality.

proof_techniques.name: Directional volume bookkeeping by coordinate projections
proof_techniques.description: Instead of estimating only the total volume of a chain, the paper decomposes volume according to coordinate directions: Vol_J(C) is the mass of the projection of C to the coordinate plane P(J). Because total volume is comparable to the sum of all directional volumes, a proof can track anisotropic information without losing control of ordinary volume. The main benefit is that fillings can be large in a few directions but small in most others, and these refined bounds survive subsequent coarea slicing.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The rectangle problem is explicitly side-length-sensitive, so tracking coordinate 2-, 3-, and 4-directional volumes is a natural way to exploit the ordered side lengths R_i and S_i rather than collapsing everything to total volume.

proof_techniques.name: Inductive absolute filling in rectangles by projection to a face
proof_techniques.description: For an absolute m-cycle in a rectangle, Proposition 2.1 fills it by first projecting to the face x_1=0 and using the straight-line homology between the cycle and its projection. The homology costs R_1 only in directions that include x_1, and the projected cycle is filled inductively in one lower dimension. The resulting estimate Vol_I(y) РЅц R_i Vol_{IРѕњi}(z), where i is the smallest coordinate in I, is sharply anisotropic and has no unnecessary dependence on long sides.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This construction can be transplanted directly to rectangular domains or to slices/fibers inside them. It is especially relevant for producing fillings whose cost is controlled by the smallest side appearing in a given coordinate direction.

proof_techniques.name: Relative boundary-pushing in rectangles via slicing
proof_techniques.description: For a relative cycle z with РѕѓzРіѓРѕѓR, Proposition 2.2 constructs a chain y with Рѕѓy=z+B and pushes the leftover boundary B into РѕѓR, while controlling directional volumes of both y and B. The proof slices z by a coordinate hyperplane chosen by coarea, subtracts a cylinder over the slice, fills the upper and lower pieces by a simple push-to-bottom/top homotopy, and handles the cylinder by induction on dimension. This is a robust way to combine relative topology with anisotropic metric estimates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original map is a map of pairs (R,РѕѓR)Рєњ(S,РѕѓS), so relative cycles and boundary terms are unavoidable. This boundary-pushing mechanism is a likely ingredient for adapting closed-manifold linking arguments to rectangles.

proof_techniques.name: Directional coarea inequality
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.1 refines the usual coarea inequality by bounding each directional volume of a fiber in terms of directional volumes of the chain being sliced. The proof inserts the weight |J┬иker dF| into the coarea formula and uses an exterior algebra identity to compare the possible direction of the kernel with the directional components of the ambient tangent plane. This lets one choose a fiber whose projections are simultaneously small in all coordinate directions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A 2-dilation bound controls Jacobians for maps to 2-dimensional targets and, via singular-value monotonicity, higher-dimensional Jacobians. Directional coarea can turn those dilation hypotheses into anisotropic bounds for fibers or intersections inside a rectangle.

proof_techniques.name: Iterated coarea-filling-coarea scheme for second-order linking
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 1 estimates L2(F) by choosing a small first fiber z_1, filling it by y_1 with directional control, choosing a small second fiber z_2 inside y_1 with directional control, filling it by y_2, and finally counting a third fiber z_3. The key improvement over ordinary isoperimetry is that the first filling y_1 concentrates directional volume in short-axis directions, which forces z_2 to have favorable directional distribution and makes the second filling cheaper.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The desired rectangle inequality resembles an obstruction obtained by testing a degree-one map against a higher-order linking invariant. The same iterative structure could produce lower bounds involving mixed products of side lengths.

proof_techniques.name: Composition with a test map and homotopy-invariant comparison
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3 proves dilation lower bounds by composing a degree-D map ╬д:EРєњE' with a carefully built map F:E'Рєњa wedge of spheres having large L2(F). Homotopy invariance and degree imply L2(FРѕў╬д)=D L2(F) up to sign, while Theorem 1 bounds L2(FРѕў╬д) from above in terms of the dilation of ╬д and the source axes. Comparing the lower and upper bounds forces a quantitative dilation lower bound.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a degree-one rectangle map f:RРєњS, one can try to construct an anisotropic test map on S with a nonzero linking invariant, compose with f, and use a source-side upper bound to force inequalities among R_i and S_i.

proof_techniques.name: Construction of large-linking test maps from thick linked spheres
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.1 builds a Lipschitz map from an ellipsoid to a glued pair of spheres by placing two thick, linked core spheres inside the double-rectangle model of the ellipsoid. One region maps by projection to a compactified rectangle sphere, and an annular linked region maps by projection to an ellipsoidal sphere. Generic fibers over the two target summands are linked once, giving linking invariant 1. Theorem 2 iterates this construction and then composes with high-degree maps on each summand to amplify L2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Rectangles contain natural products and annular regions analogous to the thick linked spheres used here. A rectangle-specific proof may need to build test maps on S whose fiber geometry reflects side lengths S_1,...,S_4.

proof_techniques.name: Double-of-a-rectangle model for ellipsoids
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses that an n-dimensional ellipsoid with axes E_0РЅц...РЅцE_n is C(n)-bilipschitz to the double of the rectangle with side lengths E_1,...,E_n. This allows rectangular directional estimates to be transferred to closed ellipsoids by treating northern and southern halves as two copies of a rectangle and managing the equatorial boundary with relative filling estimates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A map of pairs between rectangles can often be doubled to a map between closed doubles, converting relative boundary behavior into closed topology. Conversely, the paper's closed ellipsoid estimates can suggest what relative estimates should hold directly in rectangles.

proof_techniques.name: Singular-value monotonicity from k-dilation to higher dilations
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3 uses the fact that if a map has k-dilation ╬┤ and jРЅЦk, then its j-dimensional Jacobians are controlled by ╬┤^{j/k}, in the form needed to say that a composition with an L-Lipschitz map has k_1-dilation at most (╬┤^{1/k}L)^{k_1}. This follows from ordering singular values: once the product of the largest k is bounded, the remaining singular values beyond the kth are bounded by ╬┤^{1/k}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypothesis is Dil_2(f)РЅц1, but arguments may require controlling 3- or 4-dimensional Jacobians of composed maps or restrictions. This monotonicity is the standard bridge.

proof_techniques.name: Axis-order combinatorics in anisotropic product estimates
proof_techniques.description: In Theorem 1, after substituting directional coarea bounds into the second directional filling, many possible products of axes appear. The proof bounds all of them by the single product E_{nРѕњk_1+1}E_{k_3} using only the ordering E_1РЅц...РЅцE_n and the dimension identities n+2=k_1+k_2+k_3. This kind of ordered-index bookkeeping is essential for extracting clean mixed-axis inequalities from many directional terms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The target statement involves ordered side lengths and a dichotomy depending on products such as R_3R_4 and mixed powers of S_2,S_3. Similar ordered-index estimates may be needed after applying directional filling/coarea bounds in dimension 4.

other_useful_info: The paper uses constants C(n), c(n), and the notation РЅ▓ for inequalities up to a factor depending only on n; all main estimates are uniform in the eccentricities of the ellipses. It explicitly notes that the analogous problem of sharp lower bounds for the 2-dilation of degree-one maps between 4-dimensional rectangles was solved in Guth [3], described as complicated and involving nonlinear near-optimal maps; that cited work is likely the closest source for the original rectangle theorem. The paper also states, as a conjectural Euclidean analogue, that every closed k-cycle z in R^n should bound y with Vol_I(y) РЅц C(n)(╬а_{JРіѓI} Vol_J(z))^{1/k} for every coordinate (k+1)-tuple I; this conjecture is not proved in the paper but motivates the directional viewpoint. For n=4, k=k_1=k_2=k_3=2, Theorem 3 gives the explicit ellipsoid lower bound Dil_2(╬д) РЅ│ |D|^{1/3} Q_1^{1/3}Q_2^{2/3}Q_3^{2/3}Q_4^{1/3}, which is the paper's simplest stated application to dilation.
why_relevant: This paper proves anisotropic, direction-dependent isoperimetric inequalities and applies them to k-dilation lower bounds. The directional nature of the estimates is highly relevant to the side-length-sensitive rectangle inequality in the problem.
